Hey guys, JB here for your weekly intensive training. It is going to be an awesome training day. Listen, so we are on now week three of the certification program. Now for those of you who are not on certification, it's okay. You know, you should get on it because it's awesome, right? But just, you know, this is still a topic that's relevant to you. We're just following along each week now with these first 12 weeks. It's a 12 week program, very organized and regimented. And this week we are focusing on state management. And I want to just start off by saying that the reason that I put state management in this spot was because I had to do tonality, an overview of like, you know, what unconscious communication. So I want to like give you enough about like, you know, hey, here's the deal. There's tonality, by language, what's really going on in someone's mind, right? That's why I put unconscious communication before state management. And of course, the five core elements came first, right? But before I go any further here and start digging into the next two modules, which are tonality, which you get, by the way, the tonality module is ridiculously great. My graphics team spent a gazillion hours putting it together. So it's so easy to understand and so easy for you to actually internalize, right? So you're going to love that. And you'll have that. I think at the end of this week, you'll get that, right? So if that's when it comes out, it's unbelievable. It's unbelievable, right? But I wanted to make sure that before I did that, that you really not only understood the importance of having the ability to manage your own state, okay? But also that you understood that on some level that when, you know, when you're using tonality, you're changing other people's states at all as well. And that's what's happening as you move someone down the straight line, right? You're essentially taking the certainty that is inside of you. In other words, you feel absolutely certain that your product, your good, your service, that what you have to offer. And also that you, as a salesperson, as a force in this person's life, as future clients, that this person is absolutely positively want you in their life, right? You're certain about that. And that feeling of absolute certainty, it doesn't come from the straight line system. A straight line is not capable of creating certainty in someone else. The straight line system allows you to transfer the certainty that's inside of you to your prospect. You get it? So again, and hopefully I'm kind of like beating a dead horse with you. I hope you realize this, but you know, I don't think there's too many times I can say this because until you truly understand this, it's almost like you don't really know what's going on when you sell. I think one of the reasons why I was a natural one closer, why I really understood it, is in my own mind, I deeply understood that. If I want to close somebody else, I had to basically walk in their shoes. You know, I had to imagine what would this person want to be hearing, right? I almost reversed it. If I was the prospect, what would I want to hear right now? What would need to be said to me? How would it need to be said to me? What would it kind of have to look like from my perspective to want to buy something? And I keenly understood that just because how I'm built, you know, I'm empathetic and I think just being raised the way I was, whatever, the better or worse, right? And I was keenly aware that when I was communicating in a situation of influence, that I was doing what I would want to be done to me. Like how, if I was that prospect, what would I want to hear? How should the power, how would I want me to be dressing? I was able to play on both sides of the equation. And that's a really powerful thing to do to really be able to walk in someone else's shoes and to feel what they'd be feeling and to know what they want to know. You get it? And I think all natural warm closures have that ability. They have this ability to almost kind of, without thinking much about it, to just understand naturally what someone would want to hear and, you know, and what type of smile they want to be greeted with, what tonalities, right? And for me, as a salesperson, this initial root experience I had, it was the first daily motivation of the week I gave you, right? It was about this guy, the penguin. My first real sales job was in the meat and seafood industry and the penguin took me out and I watched this guy lose his state of certainty. He lost his state of certainty. He started the day off being absolutely positively, you know, it's just a bull in a Chinese shop. I bet, you know, my name is Elliot. I looked, you wouldn't want any filet mignon shrimp ball lobsters. I watched it happen. And as I've said, you don't remember everything that you see or everything you hear, but that stuck out. I was so blown away by that. I'll never forget that feeling. I was like, I wonder if he knows how terrible he sounds. Like I really wondered if he does he really know how awfully sounds because to me it was so plainly obvious. So this, so on that level, what's really happening is we have a fundamental breakdown of the ability to transfer certainty to someone else because the salesman themselves is not certain. Salesman loses his state of certainty, can't transfer it in any sales using any sales system, right? That's only one side of the equation. What I'm driving at here is that there's also the flip side, which is the fact that even if you're not selling, no matter what it is you do. If you're in a disempowered state, you can't access the resources that you have, meaning you can't tap into your best self. For example, in sports, we call that being in the zone, right? They're in the zone, athletes in the zone. It's just like they're unstoppable. They play at this level that's almost like just it's automatic or unconscious, right? They're tapping into that unconscious highest level. That's called being in the zone. That's a function of state, that's state management. And we see that with athletes all the time. And the reason we see with athletes is because when you get to this really, really high level of skill, what typically separates a Roger Federer who fucking, by the way, really disappointed me because I love Federer. He blew it with a joke of it. She's wimbled in half having two match points. I don't know if you guys watched that. It was devastating, right? I've ruined my whole Sunday, right? I'm not going to go up and down. I'm not going to lose my temper. What happens when you lose your temper? That's losing your state. You go from a state of calm to a state of anger and upset. So it's, it's, it's the whammy, it's like a double whammy here. And also, no matter how proficient you are with the actual tactics and strategies of the straight line system, if you're in a state of overwhelm and uncertainty and anger, you can't even use it well. It's like you're a sales manager or an owner's worst nightmare. You're still in that all the talent in the world, but only brings it to the table once a week or once a month and you drive them crazy. I want to go, I want to dig right into the practical side of this year with ol factory anchoring. All right. Number one, I know that it seems weird. I'm the first one to admit that, right? When I first was studying NLP. And the reason I just, you know, the reason I started studying NLP was because I'd heard that, you know, by definition, NLP is about modeling and collapsing time to make change. To me, it seemed like this. Wow. You know, if I can learn, have another tool in my toolbox, so to speak, to help people make rapid change, rapid improvements, why would that be a not an awesome thing? Right. That was my reason for learning NLP. I was very fortunate that I had Bambler as my teacher, right? Who invented NLP, right? Because frankly, without that, there's a lot of shawtons out there to teach NLP. And if it's even the slightest bit watered down, it doesn't really work. And while I'm a proponent, a major proponent of certain aspects of NLP, like belief change, especially, I was not overly impressed with the state manager, the anchoring. I found it to be lacking. And the reason was because of these two key points that Bambler raised that are just literally the ultimate necessity for truly setting an anchor. And for those of you who are not in certification, it should be, right? Here's the deal. What I'm talking about here is that anchoring on the NLP tends to break down because of two reasons. Number one, in order to really, truly set an anchor, to really lock in an anchor, you have to be at the absolute tippy top of your state. You can't be even slightly below. It's only when you're like emotionally wrought, like that, that's like your war so over the top in whatever that state might be. It could be a state of sorrow, by the way, where people get anchored into a depressed state. And it's such an intense, something happens at the best examples post traumatic stress where people will go off the war having one set of behaviors. And they'll come back and every time they hear a loud noise, they'll be, what? And they're diving under a table before they even were able to realize there was someone's in the door. Their unconscious mind triggered a memory fired off an anchor that said there's a bomb going off, run for cover. Anchors get set all the time. It's the reason why when you're driving in the car and you either hear a loud siren behind you or see a red flashing light before you know it. Your heart's beating out of your chest. Your pulse is quickened. You're breathing. You're looking around. 20 seconds later, you see an ambulance pass you by and you calm down. Oops, it wasn't a policeman. The reason that we have that response, at least most people do, is because we were kids, we were driving with our parents and a policeman pulled them over and everyone was nervous. And in that moment, you saw the fear. It's an overwhelming anxiety response. You're at that top of fear. Someone's wrong. We can get arrested. Something's going on here. And in that moment of intense fear and worry, the light is set with the stimulus happen organically. In other words, in real life, there was an actual link between feeling incredibly scared or fearful or anxious and a loud sound, noise or word. In this case, it was a visual light flashing or red light and loud sound that was organically linked to you actually being fearful because it was a fearful situation. Not just a bit. It's really fearful. I remember the first time I met my mom and I got my dad got pulled over. You should have seen that they were so worried. We were in the deep South and it was on family vacation. The worry in the car, I'm telling you, and my dad had a legitimate license, right? He was so upset and nervous about this whole thing, right? I'll never forget it. My heart was beating at myself. So now anytime I hear that and most people like me, you'll have this visual response. People that go to war, I said, right? You know, they, for better or worse, the reason was, is cause they were scared for their life. That was like, that's the ultimate fear. I'm going to die. I'm going to get blown up by a landmine. So you hear explosion, loud sound, you're flipping out. You're diving for cover. Anchors get set automatically. That's the way the world works. We have many anchors and we move through the world and we have these visual responses to the things around us based on certain things that happen. You might have a terrible, awful fear of public speaking. Why? Because the first time you tried it, you were very nervous and something happened. You had a bad first result and your heart was beating at it. You felt intense embarrassment and fear. And now even the thought of public speaking is enough to send you into an emotional tailspin. I'll be honest with you. As a child, I suffered badly from panic attacks. I really did. I had the bilitating panic attacks from the time I was like eight years old to the time I was like 15 years old. Really bad panic attacks when I was trying to go to sleep at night. Something happened and what, no one really knows what happened. But I think in retrospect what happened was there was a medication you took for nausea back before they invented the modern drugs. Now they use something called Zofran. There's another medication. I think it's called something like a strict, not strict, something that they inject into you when you're nauseous and stops vomiting. And one of the side effects of that injection is a panic attack. It can induce a panic attack. The thing that I had with the painkillers was I had profuse vomiting when I was like six or seven years old. And I took the doctor came over and gave me this injection and about an hour or two later I had a panic attack. And I linked this feeling of being panic. I was going to die with being sick. Well, up until the age of 16 when they spontaneously went away, every time I'd get sick, I would suffer from a month of panic attacks. It would bring on panic attacks. Why? Because I got linked in my brain. It linked up being sick with panic. And it took years of therapy. Seriously, I'm so screwed up. I might go use therapy for a lot of reasons. But I went to therapy as a kid for that because I had these panic attacks. And only many years later when I was getting an injection again and the doctor said, you might get an anxiety attack in this, I'm like, what? And it hit me what had happened. All those years had gone by. Seven years not understanding why every time I got sick, it would trigger a panic attack. That was why. That's why. The reality is anchors are there. I don't have to, I should have to convince you that you can set anchors. You can. You have many right now. So any lack of belief that you had before right now, please just shed that. It's not true. You can set anchors. The point with NLP is why not take something like this that can happen naturally? Why not use it to your advantage? If you can program your brain like that, why not program it to serve you? Why should it happen only if I unfortunately get panic stricken and I, I said an anchor for panic when I'm sick, not very debilitated. It'll be nice to have an anchor for feeling calm. That'd be much better. Right. NLP's breakthrough and Banler realized was you could actually program the brain artificially. What makes it not work well in the real world though is that in order to set the anchor, you got to be at the absolute tippy top of the state. You have to really be panicking. If you want to set one path, you got to really be certain to set an anchor for certainty. You can't imagine, you know, because the NLP philosophy is like, go close your eyes, go back to a moment in your life when you were feeling absolutely positively certain. That's how it starts. You search for a root experience and then you use the software of the brain. You close your eyes. Imagine how you looked in this picture in that moment. And then if you want to put a frame around that picture and now make that picture imagine like TV knobs under the picture, you can adjust the TV knobs and make the picture bigger, brighter, clearer, move it closely, all those little tricks that NLP uses to use your, to power your own brain to make images more powerful to you. Right. And on dialogue, make the dialogue louder and on new dial, create dialogue, all the sort of stuff. Right. And then get yourself like that to the tippy top of the state and then make some move because that's the second thing that causes NLP. Acquiring not to work is if you set an anchor, meaning the anchor is the actual, what is it? Is it a sound? Is it a gesture? Is it a noise? What is it? Well, you have to have something that's very, very extraordinary, not ordinary. The more extreme the better, the more uncommon the better, the more severe the better, the more it shocks your senses, the better. But that being said, what it can't be is something that shocks your senses in a negative way to create its own negative reference. In other words, like, for instance, as you know, right, I came up with this really great idea. It was a great idea to use sense of smell because that's tied directly to the limbic systems. The most basic basal responses are based on sense of smell. So reptilian, right? And it's the reason why if one, they tested this, they've tested this. If one molecule of a scent that you've smelled 30 years ago, one molecule comes and hits your old factor, you know, it instantly lights up a cascading effect of neurons in your brain. One molecule, that's how, and by the way, you know, we're like the least sensitive to this, like dogs and lab, they have dogs that sniff stuff out, you know, dogs can tell if you have cancer by smelling you. They sniff the drugs, weapons, everything, bombs, right? So animals have incredibly keen senses. We are far less keen, but still when that smell hits you, I remember, I'll never forget one of the memories I have is the smell of a freshly cut grass up in the mountains when I was a kid going to summer camp. I'll never forget that there's a certain smell that I, to this day, it puts me right back in that moment. As soon as I think about that, I almost feel the feelings and imagine the picture that happened back in the day. Same thing goes for the smell of low tide at the jetty, or my dad used to take me fishing, that salty musky smell of low tide, smell of my grandmother's house, right? That musky, morph ball scent, right? Those, those scents will be with you for the rest of your life, the rest of your life. You'll never, ever forget those. And anytime you're in a situation where you smell that, it'll take you back to that moment. You've probably experienced this yourself. It's very powerful. Well, it was somewhere about a year after I was out there teaching sales formally around the world, and I was, you know, found people struggling with state management. And I was trying to teach them NLP anchoring. And frankly, it wasn't working. Because the problem is, is that, you know, the whole idea of jumping and screaming and imagining yourself being certain doesn't really cut it. So imagine, you know, you have the certainty scale, right? So, you know, imagine yourself just closing the best sale that you ever closed. See yourself in the picture. Make that picture bigger, brighter, moving closer to you. Add on the dial. Put it into motion. See yourself in the picture. In fact, step inside your own body. So if you look at after your own eyes, it's called being in an associated state. Let those emotions wash over you. Imagine a feeling you're getting and tumble that feeling, spin that feeling. These are all the NLP techniques. And I teach them to you in, I think it was in five or six. Why do I do that? Why would I teach that to you? Well, here's the thing. Anchors are stackable. Meaning, so while you might not be able to fire off an anchor that you've kind of set from NLP, because it's not a real anchor, you didn't really get there. It does serve as a platform that you can now spring forward on to the next level. So let's say you got yourself to an eight on, let's say you go to one to a hundred. Let's say you, let's say you go a 100 on the certain. That's absolute certainty. Maybe you guys have to like a 91 or 92 using NLP techniques with imagining yourself, right? But it's not good enough. The anchor won't set unless you're at 100. That's a fact. That's an NLP is the first people who are available. Tell you that's the way it works, right? So I realized very quickly that all these subjective means of trying to get yourself to an artificially pumped up state. They just wouldn't cut. You can't imagine yourself into a state of absolute anything. Yet there are people out there that were setting anchors all the time. Why? Because it's happening to them organically. That was my break for as an open. It seems so simple when I'm like, wait a second. Why am I trying to set an anchor by imagining myself in a state of certainty? Why don't I just wait till I close my next sale? And I really am feeling that like, imagine after you close this really tough sale and you're basking in the so-called afterglow of closing that sale. You just wow, damn, did I sound good? And you feel like you can conquer the world. That's why, by the way, people will get on roles and also people getting slumps cause like success, but get success because a positive state keeps going for a while until it turns on you. Unless you can artificially manufacture one through anchoring, eventually you know you'll have some bad, you find a couple of people who are really negative and they can't, you lose your role. Next thing you know you're in a slump. See, why do slumps happen? Why would you be in a slump as an individual as a company? You know why? I'll tell you why. Slumps are part of success. Every great batter, every great tennis player, every great sales person, including me, has slumps. A lot of it has to do with the natural ebb and flow of just getting into cold areas or getting cold leads. Bad time of year. We used to have this thing we used to call strattin. We called it the Christmas crowbar cause all the stops would go down at the end of the year. It was terrible at people with you know you could, you could almost form limiting beliefs from that and lose your state of, you know, your state of certainty, right? But the point was everyone has slumps. Usually they start to not imagine. Sometimes they are. Sometimes the company that you're working for, they just might have been not been their fault. Maybe there were some bad leads came into the pipeline. Maybe, maybe the offer wasn't priced correctly. Maybe it was, it doesn't, there's as many reasons why a slump could start. But typically that little blip for a great company, alright, that's going to be very short lived and they'll fix that. They could have had a bad two days of leads, a bad week of leads happens. They realize they switch the lead source or do something they do a nip or a tuck, right? That's what people do, pivot, right? But the damage is done inadvertently because now people start closing lists. They lose self-belief. They start getting negative and what happens? Figure it out. If you show up in the state of negativity, you could have the best value of speeds. It's like Elliot that day trying to sell meat and seafood when he's knocking on doors like this. You wouldn't want any filet mignon shrimp ball lobsters. I mean like my food basically is poisonous. I'm making it like he almost was like telling him not to buy. So even people who are qualified buyers who want it, I want to be like everybody else and say no. He's telegraphing that no one wants it. Slumps are a fact of life and they usually have their causes rooted in very identifiable discernible things. Perhaps the pitch you're using was a little bit off, causing you not to close, but you fixed it. It was a little thing like it's just like for a while something happened, but then you're stuck with the slump and the slump becomes self-perpetuating. The good news is by using anchoring, by using olfactory anchoring, you can break out of any slump. Listen, what you do is you essentially take this out of the equation. You know what Djokovic said, there's so many elements I can't control. I can't control the weather. I can't control how fast the court's going to play. I can't control how Roger Federer is going to play. I can't control how Federer is going to control his emotions. I know one thing. I can't even control how good I'm playing, how's my body moving that day, but I can control my thoughts. That is in my control, not the ever, but for this match, I'm controlling my thoughts. Well, as a salesperson, adopting that philosophy is just, it makes you unstoppable because you can then use olfactory anchoring to actually make sure that you show up in your best state every single day. You break out of slumps. You know how you do it by working twice as hard and by triggering your anchor fast. So even in slumps, you're going to be closing here and there. So how do you use olfactory anchoring to set an anchor? I'm going to draw it out in the board morning shorts as high here today, right? Let me just really quickly just show you what happened very quickly. Watch. So here's what happened. NLP anchoring has got five steps. One, two, three, four, five, right? Step one is choose the state you want to anchor. Choose the state, right? Step two is choose the focus. Step three is choose your physiology. Step four is intensify your focus, your feeling by managing these two, using your brain to manipulate the image. Step five is to choose an anchor and set it. Those are the five steps that help me anchor. In other words, what state do you want to choose? I want to anchor to set an absolute certainty. Step two, all right, go back to a focus. You can choose your focus. I want you to focus on a moment in your life when you're feeling absolutely certain. Choose a sail that was a really tough sailing. You closed it. Go back to that moment. Imagine yourself there. Okay. And now imagine how you looked at that picture. How do you be standing? How are you sitting? Change your physiology right now because state is dependent on these two things. What you focus on, how you move your body triggers a state. Intensify that by using your brain, the software, make it bigger, brighter, move it closer, right? Get yourself to this absolute state of certainty, right? And then choose an anchor. Yes, or clap your hands and set the anchor. It doesn't work. It doesn't work. Why? Because it's really, really hard to get to a 10 or a 100. That's, and also, clapping your hands while shouting the word yes. It's not that unique. I mean, you know, even go to a Tony Robbins event. I love Tony, but you know, everyone's like, yes, make it. And it's not special. It's just, it's pedestrian. You need, I'll tell you what, you want an anchor? Go, go, go, smell skunk with a negative anchor. Something that's so powerful. Oh my God. Like overwhelms, shocks, the senses. So that's NLP anchor. What's olfactory anchoring? Really? Here's the brilliance. It cuts out all of this. Don't focus on anything. Don't change your physiology. Don't try to intensify it. Step one, okay? Choose the state. Step two, wait until you're there organically and pull out your boom boom and set your anchor. Wait until it happens to you naturally. See, if you have this, a portable anchor, like a boom boom, it doesn't have to be anything. Any type of smell that's portable in your pocket, right? Wait until you close a really tough sale and in that moment you're like, yes. And you know that feeling if you're in sales. You know that feeling. You just, you're just like, damn, yes. I was awesome. I could take over the world. That's being truly certain about yourself. That's a real legitimate tippy top of the scale. If you do a presentation and so in sincere, sure, and you're the lay down, that's not what I mean. I'm talking about one where you thought you were talking to everything right. Bam. You're like, yes. And you know you were great. That's what I'm talking about. In that exact moment, you take out and go, and you link the smell to that feeling of absolute certainty. And you do that once and don't use the aggregate. Do it a second time. Do it three times. You have set a really powerful anchor for certainty. Once you have that, before you pick up the phone, before you walk in a door, you could use that to trigger a state of absolute certainty and it works like a charm. Now that being said, do you have to do this every single time? No, but honestly, I don't. Why? Because I don't struggle with managing my state. I find it really, really easy. She just said rebel. I find it really easy to manage what times by way. I find it really, really easy personally to manage my state. Don't struggle every once in a while. I find myself maybe I could use a little push and I'll do it. But for the most part, I don't. But as I said in the, in the, in the search program, I said, listen, guys, I'm an oddball. I know that most people really struggle with this stuff. They find it difficult to act in a way that isn't congruent with how they feel in a sales setting. I, I swear to God, I could be literally on my sick, on my, almost on my death bed or just don't have so many problems in my life. But in that moment, when I have to show up for sales or business, I can, I can push it all aside and beat the absolute. I'm not even talking like two levels. I'm talking the top. I can be the top of my game at a moment's notice, no matter what is going on around me. Seriously, I guess if someone was really sick, it was a family. I would probably just shut down because that was what I'm saying in the general course of life. I don't mean I'm a robot, but you know what I mean? In general, the regular ups and downs, they don't impact me. They do heavily impact me outside of my business career. I'm an emotional best case as much as you. I'm the first one to admit that everyone knows me, knows I am. I'm not trying to say I'm an emotionless robot. I'm the opposite. I'm a deep feeling person. Things bother me. They ruin, they stick in my craw. Okay. So I'm not trying to say I'm, I'm, I'm lack of emotion. I'm the opposite. I'm very emotional, but I can block it all out in a moment's notice. Here's my question. Can you be honest? Can you do that? Can you bring your best self to the table every single day, day in there? And I mean, day in and day out. This guy's, you know, I always say, you know, the quote I have and say, you know, success. So much of it is about getting yourself to do the things that you know you have to do, even when you don't feel like doing them. It's a big part of success. Day in, day out to make yourself do things you don't want to do. It's easy to get yourself to do what you want to do, right? If you enjoy stuff, feels like, yeah, anyone, anyone, children do that. But adults who succeed in life, no, they get themselves to do the things they don't. Like to do and they can do it for sustained periods of time. Now that's not just about state management. Corbin Hale says, what do you do if you set up an anchor, but after a few times using it and not succeeding at the highest level for whatever reason, what do you do? Doesn't matter. If you set the anchor in a state of absolute certainty, the anchors, you just wait till the next time you close and you fire it off again. Anchors stack. Anchors are the opposite of drugs. Drugs, the more you use a drug, typically the less impact it has. It's addictive, right? You have, you know, you get tolerance. Anchors are the opposite. The more you use it, the more powerful it becomes. So just stack it. You have four bed experiences. It doesn't change the anchor, but wait to even know, you know, it might start to slowly decline over time because everything, it's an end. The smell of the freshly cut grass doesn't impact me today as much as it did 35 years ago or 50 years ago. So you'll keep refreshing, keep resetting it. You'll be fine. All right. All right.
